note: 65-year-old male presents for follow-up of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Glycemic control suboptimal with HbA1c of 8.2. Blood pressure today 152/94. Continues metformin 1000mg BID, lisinopril 20mg daily. Will increase lisinopril to 40mg and add empagliflozin 10mg.
notes: E11.65 (T2DM with hyperglycemia) preferred over E11.9 because A1c indicates active poor control.

note: Patient is a 42-year-old woman with chief complaint of right-sided lower back pain radiating to the right leg, present for 3 weeks. Denies trauma. No history of malignancy. Exam reveals positive straight leg raise on the right at 45 degrees. Diagnosis: right-sided lumbar radiculopathy.
notes: M54.41 specifically codes right-sided lumbar radiculopathy with sciatica. Note 'no history of malignancy' is negated and should not trigger any malignancy codes.

note: 28-year-old female presents with acute pharyngitis, fever to 101.4F, and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Rapid strep test positive. Started on amoxicillin 500mg TID for 10 days.
notes: J02.0 is streptococcal pharyngitis.

note: Established patient seen for annual physical. No complaints. Vitals stable. BMI 31.4. Counseled on diet and exercise. Lipid panel and HbA1c ordered.
notes: Z00.00 for routine adult medical exam without abnormal findings; E66.9 for obesity (BMI >= 30) discovered on this visit.

note: Patient admitted from ED with acute exacerbation of COPD. Productive cough, increased dyspnea over 4 days. Oxygen saturation 88% on room air, improved to 94% on 2L NC. Chest X-ray without focal consolidation. Started on prednisone, azithromycin, and bronchodilator nebulizers.
notes: J44.1 = COPD with acute exacerbation. The note rules out pneumonia (no focal consolidation), so don't code J18.9.

note: 55-year-old man post-MI 3 months ago, here for cardiac follow-up. Stable on dual antiplatelet therapy, atorvastatin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. No chest pain, no shortness of breath, no edema. Echo from last week shows EF 45%.
notes: I25.2 = old MI; I50.32 = chronic diastolic (or here, reduced EF) heart failure. The 'no chest pain' is negated and shouldn't trigger angina codes.

note: Patient with known major depressive disorder, recurrent, presents with worsening depressive symptoms over 6 weeks. PHQ-9 score 18. Denies suicidal ideation. Currently on sertraline 100mg. Increasing to 150mg and adding referral to psychotherapy.
notes: F33.2 = MDD recurrent severe without psychotic features (PHQ-9 18 = severe). Note SI is negated.

note: 12-year-old with 2-day history of right ear pain, fever, decreased appetite. Otoscopic exam: right tympanic membrane erythematous and bulging. Diagnosis: acute right otitis media. Prescribed amoxicillin.
notes: H66.91 = otitis media, unspecified, right ear. (More specific suppurative codes if pus visible, but description here is generic.)

note: 72-year-old male former smoker (45 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) presenting for quarterly COPD follow-up. Reports mild exertional dyspnea, unchanged from baseline. No acute cough, no fever, no increase in sputum production. Spirometry from last month: FEV1/FVC 0.58, FEV1 62% predicted (GOLD 2). On tiotropium 18mcg daily and albuterol PRN. O2 sat 94% on room air. Impression: stable COPD, GOLD Group B.
notes: J44.9 = COPD, unspecified — appropriate for stable disease without acute exacerbation or concurrent lower respiratory infection. No fever or increased secretions to warrant J44.0 or J44.1.

note: 68-year-old woman with chronic systolic heart failure presenting for routine cardiology follow-up. Stable at baseline; mild exertional dyspnea unchanged, no dyspnea at rest, no new lower extremity edema, weight stable. BNP 310 pg/mL, down from 480 at last visit. Echocardiogram 2 months ago demonstrated EF 33%. On furosemide 40mg daily, carvedilol 12.5mg BID, lisinopril 10mg daily, and spironolactone 25mg daily. Impression: chronic systolic heart failure, compensated.
notes: I50.22 = chronic systolic heart failure; EF 33% confirms systolic dysfunction. Patient is compensated so I50.21 (acute) is inappropriate. Use I50.23 only when acute decompensation is superimposed on a chronic baseline.

note: 34-year-old nonsmoker with moderate persistent asthma presenting for scheduled follow-up. Uses albuterol inhaler 3–4 times per week for symptomatic relief. No nighttime awakenings in the past month. Spirometry today: FEV1 79% predicted, bronchodilator response 13%. Currently on fluticasone 110mcg/actuation BID. ACT score 17 (not well controlled). No acute exacerbation today. Plan: step up to fluticasone-salmeterol combination inhaler and recheck in 6 weeks.
notes: J45.40 = moderate persistent asthma, uncomplicated. SABA use 3–4 days/week and suboptimal ACT score place this in the moderate persistent category. No exacerbation is occurring at this visit, so J45.41 (with acute exacerbation) is not appropriate.

note: 58-year-old male presenting with 5-day history of productive cough with yellow sputum, fever to 102.1F, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. No hospitalization or antibiotic use in the past 90 days. Chest X-ray: right lower lobe consolidation. WBC 14,200. O2 sat 95% on room air. Sputum culture pending. Diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia; started on azithromycin 500mg daily for 5 days.
notes: J18.9 = pneumonia, unspecified organism — correct when causative pathogen is not yet confirmed. Code updates to an organism-specific code (e.g., J13 for S. pneumoniae, J15.1 for Pseudomonas) once culture results are available.

note: 24-year-old woman presents with 2-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. No fever, no flank pain, no nausea or vomiting. Urinalysis: positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, >50 WBC/hpf. Urine culture sent. Diagnosed with uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection. Prescribed nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days.
notes: N39.0 = urinary tract infection, site not specified. No fever or costovertebral angle tenderness to suggest pyelonephritis (N10). The word 'uncomplicated' in the note is a clinical descriptor, not a separate codeable finding.

note: 52-year-old woman presenting with 3-month history of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnea. CBC: hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, MCV 71 fL (microcytic). Iron studies: ferritin 5 ng/mL, serum iron 38 mcg/dL, TIBC 432 mcg/dL. No overt GI blood loss reported. Impression: iron deficiency anemia. Started on ferrous sulfate 325mg TID. Referred to gastroenterology to evaluate for an occult source.
notes: D50.9 = iron deficiency anemia, unspecified — labs confirm classic pattern (low ferritin, low serum iron, elevated TIBC, microcytosis). Source not yet identified so an etiology-specific sub-code (e.g., D50.0 for blood loss) is not yet applicable.

note: 48-year-old woman presenting with 4-month history of fatigue, 12-pound weight gain, cold intolerance, and constipation. TSH 14.2 mIU/L (reference 0.4–4.0 mIU/L); free T4 0.6 ng/dL (low). No prior thyroid disease history. Thyroid gland non-enlarged on exam. Diagnosis: primary hypothyroidism. Initiated levothyroxine 50mcg daily; TSH to be rechecked in 6 weeks.
notes: E03.9 = hypothyroidism, unspecified — appropriate for newly diagnosed primary hypothyroidism without confirmed etiology. Anti-TPO not yet checked, so autoimmune/Hashimoto's (E06.3) and post-surgical hypothyroidism (E89.0) cannot be coded at this visit.

note: 29-year-old male presenting with a 7-month history of excessive, difficult-to-control worry about work, finances, and family. Reports associated muscle tension, irritability, difficulty concentrating, and disrupted sleep. No history of panic attacks. No substance use. GAD-7 score 14 (moderate-severe). Symptoms cause significant occupational impairment. Diagnosis: generalized anxiety disorder. Initiating sertraline 25mg with plan to titrate; referring to cognitive behavioral therapy.
notes: F41.1 = generalized anxiety disorder. Pervasive, difficult-to-control worry across multiple domains for >6 months with somatic symptoms and functional impairment satisfies DSM-5/ICD-10 criteria. No panic attacks present to code F41.0 (panic disorder).

note: 61-year-old male with type 2 diabetes mellitus presenting for follow-up. Reports 6-month history of bilateral foot numbness and burning pain, worse at night. Monofilament testing shows diminished plantar sensation bilaterally. Nerve conduction study confirms peripheral polyneuropathy. HbA1c 7.8%. Patient also screens positive for depression; PHQ-9 score 12. Known history of recurrent MDD, last episode 18 months ago. Denies suicidal ideation. Initiating duloxetine 30mg daily for both neuropathic pain and depression.
notes: E11.40 = T2DM with diabetic neuropathy, unspecified — ICD-10 combination code captures both diabetes and neuropathic complication; do not code the polyneuropathy separately with a G code. F33.1 = MDD, recurrent, moderate (PHQ-9 12). That duloxetine addresses both conditions does not affect coding.

note: 77-year-old woman with COPD (GOLD 2, stable), long-standing persistent atrial fibrillation, and chronic diastolic heart failure presenting for quarterly follow-up. No acute dyspnea, no palpitations, no worsening of lower extremity edema. Rate-controlled on diltiazem 120mg daily; INR 2.4 (therapeutic on warfarin). Echocardiogram 3 months ago: EF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction. Spirometry: FEV1/FVC 0.62, FEV1 58% predicted. On tiotropium 18mcg daily and fluticasone-vilanterol. Impression: all three chronic conditions stable.
notes: J44.9 = COPD, unspecified; stable, no exacerbation or infection. I48.11 = long-standing persistent AF (duration >12 months, not yet classified permanent). I50.32 = chronic diastolic heart failure; EF preserved but grade II diastolic dysfunction on echo establishes the diagnosis. All three are independently active and each is coded.

note: 44-year-old man referred for weight management evaluation. BMI 43.2 kg/m². Diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea 2 years ago on prior sleep study (AHI 38), currently non-adherent to CPAP. Type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 3 years ago; HbA1c 6.9% on metformin 1000mg BID, no documented diabetic complications. Lipid panel and renal function within normal limits. Referred to bariatric surgery program and re-educated on CPAP adherence.
notes: E66.01 = morbid (severe) obesity due to excess calories (BMI >= 40); more specific than E66.9. G47.33 = obstructive sleep apnea — code the condition itself; non-adherence to CPAP does not negate the diagnosis. E11.9 = T2DM without complications; A1c at goal and no diabetic complications documented.

note: 63-year-old woman with coronary artery disease (cardiac catheterization 4 years ago showing 70% LAD stenosis, managed medically without revascularization), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia presenting for annual cardiovascular review. No chest pain, no exertional dyspnea. BP 128/76 on amlodipine 10mg and ramipril 10mg. LDL 68 mg/dL on rosuvastatin 40mg. EKG: normal sinus rhythm, no ischemic changes. All conditions well controlled.
notes: I25.10 = atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery without angina pectoris; catheterization-confirmed CAD coded here. No chest pain, so angina codes (I25.11x) are not appropriate. I10 = essential hypertension. E78.5 = hyperlipidemia, unspecified. All three are chronic conditions active at this visit.

note: 19-year-old college student with longstanding mild persistent asthma, allergic rhinitis, and atopic dermatitis presenting for combined allergy and pulmonology follow-up. Uses albuterol 1–2 times per week, no nighttime awakenings; on fluticasone 110mcg/actuation BID. ACT score 20 (well controlled), no exacerbation today. Seasonal nasal congestion and sneezing controlled on loratadine 10mg daily; specific allergen not confirmed by testing. Eczema patches on bilateral antecubital fossae, managed with triamcinolone 0.1% ointment.
notes: J45.30 = mild persistent asthma, uncomplicated; SABA 1–2x/week is mild persistent, no exacerbation at this visit. J30.9 = allergic rhinitis, unspecified; seasonal pattern noted but allergen not confirmed by testing, so a specific allergen code (e.g., J30.1) is not appropriate. L20.9 = atopic dermatitis, unspecified.

note: 69-year-old man presenting with 2-day history of acute swelling, erythema, and severe pain of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint. Gout attributed to impaired urate excretion from chronic kidney disease. Serum uric acid 9.2 mg/dL. eGFR 36 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD stage 3b). History of hypertension, well controlled on amlodipine 5mg. Colchicine 0.6mg BID initiated; allopurinol dose adjusted for renal function. Impression: acute gout flare of right foot due to renal impairment, hypertensive CKD stage 3b.
notes: M10.371 = gout due to renal impairment, right ankle and foot — ICD-10 provides an etiology-specific gout code when CKD is the established cause; do not use idiopathic gout code M10.071. I12.9 = hypertensive CKD stage 1–4; ICD-10 presumes a causal link when hypertension and CKD coexist — code I12.9, not separate I10 + N18.x. N18.32 = CKD stage 3b (eGFR 30–44 mL/min) required as an additional code alongside I12.9.

note: 82-year-old woman brought from a nursing facility with 1-day history of acute confusion, fever to 103.2F, and heart rate 118. Urinalysis: >100 WBC/hpf, positive nitrites. Empiric piperacillin-tazobactam started. Blood and urine cultures drawn; blood cultures finalized at 48 hours growing Escherichia coli. Lactate 1.4 mmol/L. SOFA score 2. Impression: sepsis due to E. coli with urinary tract source.
notes: A41.51 = sepsis due to Escherichia coli — organism-specific code applied once cultures confirm E. coli; preferred over A41.9 (unspecified). N39.0 = UTI, coded as the infectious source and sequenced after the sepsis code per ICD-10-CM sepsis sequencing guidelines. E. coli bacteremia is not coded separately when sepsis is the principal diagnosis.

note: 51-year-old male with 20-year history of heavy daily alcohol use presenting with increasing abdominal girth and fatigue. Continues to consume approximately 8–10 drinks daily; meets DSM-5 criteria for severe alcohol use disorder. Abdominal ultrasound: nodular liver consistent with cirrhosis, splenomegaly; no ascites. Platelets 68,000/uL (low), consistent with hypersplenism from portal hypertension. AST 92, ALT 47, total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL. Referred to hepatology; alcohol cessation counseled.
notes: F10.20 = alcohol use disorder, moderate/severe, uncomplicated (no concurrent withdrawal or intoxication episode coded here). K70.30 = alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without ascites; ascites absent on imaging distinguishes K70.30 from K70.31. D69.59 = other secondary thrombocytopenia; hypersplenism from portal hypertension is the mechanism, making this more precise than D69.6 (unspecified thrombocytopenia).

note: 71-year-old male with a 14-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension presenting to nephrology for CKD management. HbA1c 8.9% (suboptimally controlled). Blood pressure 148/88 on lisinopril 40mg daily. Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD stage 3b). Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 310 mg/g, consistent with moderately increased albuminuria and diabetic nephropathy. Metformin held due to eGFR <45; insulin glargine titrated. Adding empagliflozin 10mg for renal protection. Impression: type 2 diabetes mellitus with poor glycemic control, hypertensive CKD stage 3b.
notes: E11.65 = T2DM with hyperglycemia (HbA1c 8.9% confirms inadequate glycemic control). When CKD coexists with both T2DM and hypertension, ICD-10-CM guidelines require coding both conditions; I12.9 = hypertensive CKD, stage 1–4 (ICD-10 presumes a causal link between HTN and CKD — do not code I10 separately when I12.9 is present). N18.32 = CKD stage 3b (eGFR 30–44 mL/min) is required as an additional code alongside I12.9.

note: 74-year-old male with chronic systolic heart failure (baseline EF 35%) and known persistent atrial fibrillation on apixaban, presenting with a 5-day history of 14-pound weight gain, worsening dyspnea at rest, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Blood pressure 162/94 on current outpatient regimen. Bibasilar crackles on auscultation. BNP 2,450 pg/mL, up from baseline of 440 pg/mL. Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly with bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion. Admitted for IV diuresis with furosemide. Impression: acute decompensation of chronic systolic heart failure, with persistent atrial fibrillation and hypertension as contributing comorbidities.
notes: I50.23 = acute on chronic systolic heart failure; patient has a documented chronic systolic baseline (EF 35%) with superimposed acute decompensation — this is more precise than I50.21 (purely acute) or I50.22 (purely chronic). I48.19 = other persistent atrial fibrillation (≥7 days but <12 months); if duration exceeds 12 months, I48.11 (long-standing persistent) would apply instead. I10 = essential hypertension coded separately as an independently active comorbidity contributing to decompensation.

note: 79-year-old male with an 8-year history of Parkinson's disease on carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 TID, presenting with progressive cognitive decline over 18 months. Family reports worsening short-term memory, executive dysfunction, and visuospatial difficulties. MMSE 17/30. Videofluoroscopic swallow study demonstrates oropharyngeal dysphagia with silent aspiration of thin liquids. Rivastigmine transdermal patch 9.5 mg/24hr initiated for Parkinson's disease dementia. Referred to speech-language pathology; diet modified to nectar-thick liquids. Impression: Parkinson's disease dementia with oropharyngeal dysphagia.
notes: G20 = Parkinson's disease, sequenced first as the underlying etiology. F02.80 = dementia in other diseases classified elsewhere, without behavioral disturbance — ICD-10-CM mandates the etiology code (G20) before the dementia manifestation code (F02.80); they are not interchangeable. R13.10 = dysphagia, unspecified — coded separately as it is an active, clinically significant complication confirmed on VFSS and independently managed with dietary modification and SLP referral.

note: 32-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed 4 years ago, presenting for nephrology follow-up after kidney biopsy 3 months ago confirming lupus nephritis class III (focal proliferative glomerulonephritis). eGFR 64 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD stage 2). Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 1.1 g/g. Blood pressure 146/92 on amlodipine 5mg (newly started). Complement C3 low; dsDNA antibody titer elevated. On hydroxychloroquine 400mg daily, mycophenolate mofetil 1500mg BID, and prednisone 10mg daily. Impression: SLE with active proliferative lupus nephritis, CKD stage 2, and hypertension.
notes: M32.14 = systemic lupus erythematosus with glomerulonephritis — ICD-10 combination code captures both the SLE and its glomerular manifestation; do not code lupus nephritis separately with a standalone glomerulonephritis code. N18.2 = CKD stage 2 (eGFR 60–89 mL/min); biopsy-confirmed kidney injury supports coding CKD despite the relatively preserved eGFR. I10 = essential hypertension; coded separately as a new, independently managed comorbidity requiring its own treatment.

note: 59-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 due to longstanding diabetic nephropathy (eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73m²), presenting to nephrology for management of CKD-related complications. Hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL; reticulocyte count low; iron studies normal — pattern consistent with anemia of CKD. On darbepoetin alfa 40 mcg SC every 2 weeks. Intact PTH 430 pg/mL (elevated); calcium 9.0 mg/dL, phosphorus 5.9 mg/dL. Starting cinacalcet 30mg daily for secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin. Not yet on dialysis. Impression: CKD stage 4 with anemia of chronic kidney disease and secondary hyperparathyroidism.
notes: N18.4 = CKD stage 4 (eGFR 15–29 mL/min); eGFR 24 places this squarely in stage 4. D63.1 = anemia in chronic kidney disease — the specific combination code when anemia is attributable to CKD; do not use D64.9 (unspecified anemia) when CKD is the established cause. N25.81 = secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin; elevated PTH driven by CKD-related phosphate retention and reduced calcitriol synthesis is coded here, not E21.1 (secondary hyperparathyroidism, not elsewhere classified), which is reserved for non-renal causes.

note: 54-year-old male with severe alcohol use disorder (approximately 12 drinks daily) and known alcoholic cirrhosis, presenting to the ED with confusion and increasing abdominal girth over 3 weeks. Examination: asterixis present bilaterally; abdominal shifting dullness. Ammonia 126 μmol/L (elevated). Diagnostic and therapeutic paracentesis: 2.8L drained; SAAG 1.3 g/dL consistent with portal hypertension. INR 1.8, total bilirubin 3.4 mg/dL. Lactulose and rifaximin started; patient admitted. No evidence of acute GI bleeding. Impression: alcoholic cirrhosis with ascites and alcoholic hepatic failure with encephalopathy, without coma.
notes: F10.20 = alcohol use disorder, moderate or severe, uncomplicated; no concurrent withdrawal or acute intoxication episode is documented at this visit. K70.31 = alcoholic cirrhosis of liver with ascites; paracentesis-confirmed ascites distinguishes K70.31 from K70.30 (without ascites) — compare with ex024. K70.40 = alcoholic hepatic failure without coma; asterixis and elevated ammonia confirm hepatic encephalopathy, but the patient retains consciousness — use K70.41 only if coma is documented.

note: 61-year-old woman with a 12-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on chronic low-dose prednisone 5mg daily, presenting with progressive exertional dyspnea over 4 months. HRCT chest: bilateral basilar reticulation and honeycombing consistent with UIP pattern attributed to RA-associated interstitial lung disease. PFTs: FVC 68%, DLCO 55% predicted. CBC: hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL; ferritin 320 ng/mL, serum iron low — pattern consistent with anemia of chronic inflammation. DXA scan: lumbar spine T-score –2.7 (osteoporosis) attributed to chronic glucocorticoid use. RF positive, anti-CCP 380 U/mL. Initiating pirfenidone for RA-ILD and alendronate for glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis.
notes: M05.10 = seropositive rheumatoid arthritis with rheumatoid lung disease, unspecified site — ICD-10 provides this combination code when seropositive RA involves the lungs; coding RA and ILD separately would be less precise and incorrect. D63.8 = anemia in other chronic diseases classified elsewhere; elevated ferritin with low serum iron in the setting of active RA confirms anemia of chronic inflammation — more specific than D64.9 (unspecified anemia). M81.4 = drug-induced osteoporosis without current pathological fracture; chronic glucocorticoid therapy is the documented mechanism, distinguishing this from age-related osteoporosis (M81.0).

note: 70-year-old male with severe COPD (GOLD stage 3; FEV1 38% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.52) presenting for cardiopulmonary follow-up. Reports dyspnea on minimal exertion. O2 saturation 91% on 2L nasal cannula at rest. Right heart catheterization (last month): mean pulmonary arterial pressure 40 mmHg, pulmonary vascular resistance 5.1 Wood units. Echocardiogram: RV dilation, RV hypertrophy, and interventricular septal flattening consistent with chronic cor pulmonale. On tiotropium, fluticasone-salmeterol, and supplemental oxygen. Ambrisentan 5mg initiated for pulmonary hypertension. No acute exacerbation at this visit. Impression: severe stable COPD with secondary pulmonary hypertension and chronic cor pulmonale.
notes: J44.9 = COPD, unspecified — stable at this visit with no acute exacerbation or concurrent lower respiratory infection; J44.1 would require documented exacerbation. I27.29 = other secondary pulmonary hypertension; PHT due to COPD-related chronic hypoxia is secondary and coded here — not the primary pulmonary arterial hypertension codes (I27.21). I27.81 = cor pulmonale, chronic — RV dilation and hypertrophy confirmed on echo and RHC warrant this as a distinct additional code; do not assume it is subsumed under I27.29.

note: 45-year-old male presenting to the ED with acute onset left-sided chest pain radiating to the left arm, prompting concern for acute coronary syndrome. Serial troponin I at 0 and 6 hours both undetectable (<0.04 ng/mL). EKG: normal sinus rhythm, no ST-segment elevation, no T-wave inversions, no new Q waves. Chest X-ray unremarkable. Pain reproducible on palpation of the left 3rd and 4th costochondral junctions. No diaphoresis, no dyspnea. Aspirin and heparin held after negative cardiac workup. Diagnosed with costochondritis. Discharged with ibuprofen 600mg TID.
notes: M94.0 = chondrocostal junction syndrome (costochondritis). ACS is explicitly ruled out by undetectable serial troponins and a normal EKG — do not code I21.x (STEMI/NSTEMI) or I20.x (angina). Left arm radiation is part of the presenting complaint that triggered the workup, not an independently codeable finding. The negative cardiac workup makes any ischemic heart disease code inappropriate regardless of the alarming initial presentation.

note: 28-year-old woman with three prior similar episodes in the past month presenting to the ED with sudden onset shortness of breath, chest tightness, palpitations, and lightheadedness at rest. Heart rate 122, O2 saturation 99% on room air. D-dimer 0.41 μg/mL (borderline); CT pulmonary angiography performed: no filling defect, no evidence of pulmonary embolism. EKG: sinus tachycardia only, no right heart strain pattern, no S1Q3T3. Symptoms resolved with controlled breathing and reassurance within 35 minutes. Diagnosed with panic disorder; referred to psychiatry for outpatient management.
notes: F41.0 = panic disorder (recurrent unexpected panic attacks with persistent concern, confirmed by three episodes in one month). CT-PA explicitly rules out pulmonary embolism — do not code I26.09 or any I26.x code. A negative imaging study must be honored; the ruled-out condition is not coded. Sinus tachycardia during an acute panic episode is a physiologic response and is not coded separately. Dyspnea and chest tightness are symptoms subsumed into the panic disorder diagnosis.

note: 19-year-old male with a 3-day history of sore throat, low-grade fever to 100.2F, and mild bilateral anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. No tonsillar exudate. Rapid streptococcal antigen test: negative. Throat culture sent and finalized at 48 hours: no growth. Monospot (heterophile antibody) test: negative. No antibiotic prescribed. Supportive care with acetaminophen and fluids. Diagnosis: acute viral pharyngitis.
notes: J02.9 = acute pharyngitis, unspecified — appropriate when no causative organism is confirmed. Negative rapid strep test and negative throat culture explicitly rule out streptococcal pharyngitis (J02.0); do not assign J02.0 without microbiologic confirmation. Negative monospot rules out EBV infectious mononucleosis (B27.09) — do not code it. Lymphadenopathy and low-grade fever are signs of the pharyngitis and are not coded separately when they are part of the same acute presentation.

note: 16-year-old male presenting with a 2-day history of right lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, and low-grade fever to 38.0°C. WBC 11,800 (mildly elevated). CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: appendix fully visualized, normal caliber, no periappendiceal fat stranding or free fluid; multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes measuring up to 1.5 cm in the right lower quadrant. Surgical consultation obtained; appendicitis excluded. Diagnosed with acute nonspecific mesenteric lymphadenitis. Managed conservatively with IV fluids and analgesics; discharged home with return precautions.
notes: I88.0 = nonspecific mesenteric lymphadenitis — CT identifies enlarged mesenteric nodes as the cause of RLQ pain with the appendix explicitly visualized and normal. Appendicitis (K37 unspecified, or K35.x acute) is excluded by imaging and surgical consultation; do not code it. Abdominal pain (R10.31) is not coded separately when it is the presenting symptom of a confirmed diagnosis. Mild leukocytosis is a laboratory finding consistent with the lymphadenitis and is not independently coded.

note: 58-year-old woman presenting with a 4-day history of right lower leg erythema, warmth, swelling, and tenderness with a well-demarcated advancing border. Temperature 38.3°C, WBC 13,600. Compression duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity performed to evaluate for deep vein thrombosis: all deep veins fully compressible with normal augmentation; no intraluminal thrombus identified. Diagnosed with right lower leg cellulitis. Started on cephalexin 500mg QID for 7 days with instructions to return if spreading or no improvement at 48 hours.
notes: L03.115 = cellulitis of right lower limb. Compression duplex ultrasound explicitly rules out deep vein thrombosis — do not code I82.4x1 or any DVT code. Erythema, warmth, and swelling are cardinal signs of cellulitis and are not coded separately when they are defining features of the established diagnosis. Fever (R50.9) is a sign of the infectious process and is not coded as a separate diagnosis alongside cellulitis.

note: 67-year-old male presenting with sudden onset left arm weakness and slurred speech lasting approximately 25 minutes, with complete spontaneous resolution before ED arrival. Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage, no hyperdensity, no obvious infarct. MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging: no restricted diffusion, no acute ischemic lesion identified. Carotid duplex: mild bilateral atherosclerosis, no hemodynamically significant stenosis. EKG: normal sinus rhythm. Neurology consulted. Aspirin 325mg started; admitted for stroke risk stratification including telemetry and echocardiogram. Diagnosis: transient ischemic attack.
notes: G45.9 = transient ischemic attack, unspecified. MRI DWI explicitly rules out acute ischemic stroke (I63.x) — infarction is not coded without imaging confirmation of tissue injury. The neurological symptoms that fully resolved before imaging are classic for TIA, not stroke. Left arm weakness (R20.x) and dysarthria (R47.81) are symptoms of the TIA episode and are not coded separately when TIA is the established diagnosis.

note: 34-year-old woman with a known history of migraines presenting to the ED with the 'worst headache of her life,' prompting concern for subarachnoid hemorrhage. Non-contrast CT head: no intracranial hemorrhage, no hyperdensity in subarachnoid spaces. Lumbar puncture performed: opening pressure 17 cm H2O; CSF clear and colorless, no xanthochromia, RBC 3/mm³ (attributed to traumatic tap), WBC 1/mm³, glucose 64 mg/dL (normal), protein 38 mg/dL (normal). No fever, no neck stiffness, no photophobia beyond her typical migraine pattern. Symptom character consistent with prior severe migraines. Discharged with sumatriptan 100mg and outpatient neurology follow-up.
notes: G43.909 = migraine, unspecified, not intractable, without status migrainosus. CT and LP explicitly rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage (I60.x) — do not code it; the chief complaint 'worst headache ever' is a presenting symptom, not a diagnosis. Normal CSF (no xanthochromia, no pleocytosis, normal glucose/protein) rules out bacterial meningitis (G00.x) and viral meningitis (G02) — do not code either. The negative workup supports returning to the patient's established migraine diagnosis.

note: 22-year-old male presenting after a witnessed loss of consciousness lasting approximately 30 seconds following prolonged standing at a crowded outdoor event. No tonic-clonic movements, no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, no post-ictal confusion; returned to baseline immediately on lying down. EKG: normal sinus rhythm, QTc 408 ms, no delta waves, no Brugada pattern. No prior cardiac history, no family history of sudden cardiac death. Orthostatic vitals: systolic BP drops 20 mmHg on standing. Forty-eight-hour Holter monitor: no significant arrhythmia detected, no pauses. Diagnosed with vasovagal syncope. Counseled on precipitating triggers and postural maneuvers.
notes: R55 = syncope and collapse — the correct code for vasovagal syncope; ICD-10-CM does not have a more specific code for vasovagal etiology. Holter monitor and EKG rule out arrhythmia (I47.x SVT, I48.x AFib, I44.x heart block) — do not code without evidence. Normal QTc and absence of Brugada pattern rule out long QT syndrome (I45.81) and Brugada syndrome — do not code them. Absence of tonic-clonic activity, tongue biting, and post-ictal phase rules out seizure/epilepsy (G40.x) — do not code it. Orthostatic hypotension (I95.1) elicited on exam is a physiologic finding consistent with vasovagal syncope and is not coded as a separate diagnosis.

note: 68-year-old woman presenting with a 2-year history of left knee pain, worse with stair climbing and prolonged standing, with morning stiffness lasting under 30 minutes. Right knee asymptomatic and unremarkable on examination. Left knee: crepitus throughout range of motion, mild effusion, bony joint-line enlargement. Weight-bearing X-rays of the left knee: medial compartment joint space narrowing, marginal osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis. Right knee X-ray not obtained. Diagnosed with primary osteoarthritis of the left knee. Prescribed naproxen 500mg BID and referred to physical therapy.
notes: M17.12 = primary osteoarthritis, left knee. The right knee is explicitly asymptomatic and normal on examination — do not code M17.11 (right) or M17.0 (bilateral). Do not default to the unspecified code M17.10 when the affected side is documented; ICD-10-CM assigns the 6th character for laterality: 1 = right, 2 = left, with M17.0 reserved for confirmed bilateral disease.

note: 72-year-old woman presenting after a fall on an outstretched right hand. X-rays of the right wrist: dorsally displaced and angulated fracture of the distal radius consistent with a Colles fracture; no intra-articular extension. Left wrist examined and found without tenderness, deformity, or swelling. Closed reduction performed in the ED under hematoma block; placed in a sugar-tong splint. Orthopedic follow-up scheduled in 5 days. Initial encounter for closed fracture.
notes: S52.531A = Colles' fracture of right radius, initial encounter for closed fracture. The left wrist is explicitly examined and normal — do not code a left-sided fracture. Do not use S52.539A (unspecified radius) when right-sided laterality is documented in the note. The 7th character 'A' denotes initial encounter (active treatment phase); use 'D' for subsequent encounters and 'S' for sequela. Colles' fracture has a dedicated ICD-10 code (S52.53x) — do not use the generic lower-end radius fracture code S52.501x when the Colles pattern is specified.

note: 54-year-old male carpenter with a 3-month history of left shoulder pain and weakness with overhead activity. Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy impingement tests positive on the left. Right shoulder: full painless range of motion, negative impingement signs. MRI left shoulder: partial-thickness articular-surface supraspinatus tendon tear with subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, consistent with rotator cuff syndrome. Left shoulder X-ray: no fracture, no acromioclavicular pathology. Diagnosed with left shoulder rotator cuff syndrome. Referred for subacromial corticosteroid injection and physical therapy.
notes: M75.42 = impingement syndrome of left shoulder. In ICD-10-CM editions that classify M75.1x as rotator cuff tear/rupture codes rather than syndrome codes, the provider's diagnosis of 'rotator cuff syndrome' maps to M75.42 (Impingement syndrome of left shoulder), which is documented by positive Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy tests and subacromial bursitis. The right shoulder is explicitly normal — do not code M75.41 (right). A partial-thickness supraspinatus tear, if coded separately, would be M75.112 (Incomplete rotator cuff tear, left shoulder), but the provider uses 'rotator cuff syndrome' as the unifying diagnosis here.

note: 74-year-old male with a 4-year progressive history of bilateral groin and hip pain with ambulation and weight-bearing, affecting both sides. X-rays of both hips: symmetric loss of superior joint space, marginal osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis bilaterally, right slightly greater than left. No prior hip fractures, steroid use, or other secondary cause identified. Diagnosed with bilateral primary osteoarthritis of the hips. Referred to orthopedic surgery for evaluation for bilateral total hip arthroplasty.
notes: M16.0 = bilateral primary osteoarthritis of hip — ICD-10-CM provides a dedicated bilateral code; use it when both hips are confirmed affected rather than coding each side separately. Do not assign M16.11 (right) and M16.12 (left) as separate codes when a single bilateral combination code exists; M16.0 is more precise. Do not use M16.10 (unspecified laterality) when bilateral involvement is explicitly documented. Asymmetric severity (right slightly worse) does not change the bilateral classification.

note: 23-year-old male presenting after inverting his left ankle while landing from a jump during a basketball game. Tenderness and mild swelling localized to the left anterior talofibular ligament region. Right ankle: no tenderness, no swelling, full range of motion. Ottawa ankle rules applied; X-rays of the left ankle show no fracture. Neurovascular exam intact distally. Diagnosis: sprain of the left ankle. Applied compression wrap, ice, and elevation; prescribed ibuprofen 400mg TID. Initial encounter.
notes: S93.402A = sprain of unspecified ligament of left ankle, initial encounter. The right ankle is explicitly normal — do not code a right-sided injury. Do not use S93.409A (unspecified ankle) when left-sided laterality is documented. The 7th character 'A' indicates initial encounter for active treatment. 'Unspecified ligament' (S93.40x) is appropriate here because the note identifies the ATFL region clinically but does not specify the individual ligament by name in the final diagnosis; if the provider specifies ATFL, a more granular code within S93.4 would apply.

note: 45-year-old male carpenter with a 6-week history of right elbow lateral pain, exacerbated by wrist extension and power gripping. Point tenderness over the right lateral epicondyle; Cozen test (resisted wrist extension) reproduces pain on the right. Left elbow: no tenderness, full painless range of motion. X-ray right elbow: no fracture, no loose bodies. Diagnosed with right lateral epicondylitis. Initiated eccentric wrist-extensor stretching, fitted with a forearm counterforce brace, and started on naproxen 500mg BID.
notes: M77.11 = lateral epicondylitis, right elbow. The left elbow is explicitly normal — do not code M77.12 (left). Do not use M77.10 (unspecified elbow) when the affected side is documented. Lateral epicondylitis (M77.1x) must be distinguished from medial epicondylitis or 'golfer's elbow' (M77.0x); both share the same laterality suffix convention (1 = right, 2 = left). Tenderness and a positive Cozen test are clinical findings supporting the diagnosis and are not independently coded.

note: 52-year-old woman with a 5-month history of bilateral hand numbness and tingling predominantly in the thumb, index, and middle fingers of both hands, worse at night and with prolonged keyboard use. Phalen test positive bilaterally; Tinel sign positive at both wrists. Nerve conduction studies: prolonged median nerve distal motor latency bilaterally (right 4.9 ms, left 5.2 ms; reference <4.4 ms) with reduced sensory conduction velocities bilaterally. Diagnosed with bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome. Bilateral nocturnal wrist splints prescribed; referred to hand surgery for consideration of bilateral carpal tunnel release.
notes: G56.03 = carpal tunnel syndrome, bilateral upper limbs. ICD-10-CM added G56.03 as a dedicated bilateral code (FY2021); use it when both upper limbs are confirmed affected. Do not use G56.00 (unspecified laterality) or code G56.01 + G56.02 separately when G56.03 is available — the bilateral code is more precise. Contrast with ex041 (M17.12, left knee OA) where the correct code is laterality-specific because the contralateral knee is explicitly documented as normal.

note: 29-year-old male presenting with acute right knee pain, swelling, and intermittent locking after a pivoting injury during a soccer match. McMurray test produces pain and a palpable click on the medial side of the right knee. Valgus and varus stress tests negative bilaterally; anterior and posterior drawer tests negative bilaterally. Left knee: no effusion, full range of motion, no meniscal provocation signs. MRI right knee: complete bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus with peripheral fragment displacement into the intercondylar notch. Left knee MRI not performed. Referred to orthopedic surgery for right knee arthroscopic meniscal repair. Initial encounter.
notes: S83.211A = bucket-handle tear of medial meniscus, current injury, right knee, initial encounter. The left knee is explicitly examined and normal — do not code a left-sided injury. Do not use S83.201A (unspecified meniscus) when MRI and McMurray testing identify the medial meniscus specifically. Do not use M23.211 (derangement of medial meniscus due to old tear) — this is an acute traumatic injury coded in the S83 injury chapter; M23.2x codes are reserved for chronic derangements from prior tears. The 7th character 'A' denotes initial encounter for active treatment.

note: Lab results released for patient seen 3 days ago for routine wellness visit. Fasting plasma glucose 112 mg/dL (reference: <100 normal, 100–125 impaired fasting glucose, ≥126 diagnostic for diabetes). HbA1c 6.3% (reference: <5.7% normal, 5.7–6.4% pre-diabetes range, ≥6.5% diagnostic for diabetes). CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and lipid panel all within normal limits. No provider assessment, impression, or diagnosis statement documented at time of results review. Patient notified; follow-up appointment to be scheduled.
notes: R73.01 = impaired fasting glucose — the fasting plasma glucose of 112 mg/dL falls in the 100–125 mg/dL range, which maps to this code. R73.09 = other abnormal glucose — covers the abnormal HbA1c value in the absence of a provider-documented diagnosis. Cannot code E11.x (T2DM) or R73.03 (pre-diabetes) without an explicit provider diagnosis statement; the lab reference range label ('pre-diabetes range') in the result report is not a provider diagnosis. Do not code E11.65 or any diabetes code when the HbA1c is below 6.5% and no diagnosis has been documented.

note: 38-year-old woman presenting with a 3-month history of persistent generalized fatigue and low energy. CBC: hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, MCV 88 fL (normal). Comprehensive metabolic panel: normal. TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal). Fasting glucose 92 mg/dL (normal). HbA1c 5.4% (normal). Ferritin 28 ng/mL (normal). Vitamin B12 and vitamin D within normal limits. PHQ-9 score 3 (minimal). Sleep history unremarkable; no snoring or witnessed apneas. Physical examination without abnormality. Impression: fatigue, etiology not identified. Lifestyle counseling provided; return in 6 weeks.
notes: R53.83 = other fatigue — coded as the presenting symptom when no underlying etiology is identified after a thorough workup. Do not code any of the conditions that were tested for and returned normal: anemia (D64.9), hypothyroidism (E03.9), diabetes (E11.x), depression (F32.x), or iron deficiency (D50.9) — a negative workup does not make a diagnosis. R53.82 (chronic fatigue, not CFS) or G93.32 (myalgic encephalomyelitis/CFS) would require the provider to document those specific diagnoses with fulfilled clinical criteria; they cannot be inferred from symptom duration alone.

note: 57-year-old woman with a history of left breast cancer (infiltrating ductal carcinoma, T2N1M0, Stage IIB) diagnosed 6 years ago, treated with left modified radical mastectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy, and radiation therapy; completed treatment 5 years ago. Presenting for annual oncology surveillance visit. No new breast complaints. Right breast examination: no mass, no skin changes. Recent right mammogram: no suspicious findings. Chest CT and bone scan from last month: no evidence of recurrence or metastatic disease. Impression: breast cancer, no evidence of recurrence. Continue annual surveillance.
notes: Z85.3 = personal history of malignant neoplasm of breast — used when treatment is complete and there is no evidence of active disease; do not code C50.x (active malignancy) when the provider documents no evidence of recurrence. Z08 = encounter for follow-up examination after completed treatment for malignant neoplasm — captures the surveillance purpose of the visit. Do not code the original TNM staging or the prior surgical/treatment procedures as current diagnoses. If active recurrence or metastasis were documented, C50.x or C79.x would replace Z85.3.

note: 56-year-old male with longstanding chronic low back pain from lumbar degenerative disc disease, managed by a pain management specialist. Oxycodone 5mg every 6 hours as needed for breakthrough pain prescribed alongside gabapentin 300mg TID and a structured physical therapy program. PDMP review: no controlled substance overlap or early refill patterns. No documentation of aberrant drug-seeking behavior. Provider explicitly documents that DSM-5 criteria for opioid use disorder are not met. Impression: chronic low back pain; continue current multimodal pain regimen.
notes: M54.50 = low back pain, unspecified — the provider-documented diagnosis driving the visit. Z79.891 = long-term current use of opiate analgesic — appropriate when opioids are legitimately prescribed for chronic pain management without a documented opioid use disorder. Do not code F11.20 (opioid use disorder, moderate or severe) from the presence of an opioid prescription alone; OUD requires explicit provider documentation meeting DSM-5 criteria. Do not code an epilepsy (G40.x) or neuropathy (G62.9) diagnosis for the gabapentin without a documented indication — medications cannot be used to infer diagnoses.

note: 67-year-old male presenting with fever, productive cough, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. CT pulmonary angiography with contrast performed to evaluate for pulmonary embolism: no pulmonary embolism identified. Right lower lobe consolidation consistent with community-acquired pneumonia. Incidental 5mm right upper lobe solid pulmonary nodule noted; radiologist recommends follow-up low-dose CT chest in 12 months per Fleischner Society guidelines. No prior CT available for comparison. Azithromycin and amoxicillin-clavulanate started for pneumonia. Pulmonary nodule discussed with patient.
notes: J18.9 = pneumonia, unspecified organism — the primary reason for the encounter, confirmed on CT; organism-specific codes apply once culture results are available. R91.1 = solitary pulmonary nodule — the incidental finding is clinically documented, discussed with the patient, and carries a follow-up recommendation, so it is coded alongside the primary diagnosis. Do not omit incidental findings when the provider acknowledges them and outlines a management plan. Do not code I26.x (pulmonary embolism) — it was explicitly ruled out by CT-PA.

note: 42-year-old male presenting with a 3-week history of persistent low mood, markedly diminished interest in usual hobbies, poor sleep, and reduced energy. PHQ-9 score 7 (mild range, 5–9). No prior episodes of depression. Reports mild impairment in work performance but continues to fulfill occupational and social responsibilities. No psychotic features. Denies suicidal ideation or homicidal ideation. Diagnosis: major depressive disorder, single episode, mild severity. Initiating weekly cognitive behavioral therapy; pharmacotherapy deferred pending reassessment at 6-week follow-up.
notes: F32.0 = major depressive disorder, single episode, mild. PHQ-9 of 7 falls in the mild range (5–9); F32.1 requires 10–14 (moderate) and F32.2 requires 15–19 (severe without psychotic features). 'Single episode' is documented — use F32.x (single episode), not F33.x (recurrent, which requires two or more lifetime episodes). Do not default to F32.9 (MDD single episode, unspecified severity) when the provider explicitly characterizes severity; the coder must reflect the documented severity, not assume unspecified. Compare with ex017 (F33.1, recurrent moderate) and ex007 (F33.2, recurrent severe).

note: 64-year-old male with known CKD stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, baseline eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m²) presenting with 2-day history of decreased urine output and malaise after starting ibuprofen for knee pain one week ago. Creatinine today 3.2 mg/dL, eGFR 19 mL/min/1.73m². BUN 58 mg/dL. Urine sodium 6 mEq/L, fractional excretion of sodium <1%, consistent with prerenal etiology. Ibuprofen discontinued. IV normal saline initiated. Impression: acute kidney injury superimposed on chronic kidney disease stage 3a, likely NSAID-induced prerenal.
notes: N17.9 = acute kidney injury, unspecified — the acute deterioration from the documented baseline is coded with N17.x. N18.31 = CKD stage 3a — the underlying baseline CKD is coded from the patient's documented baseline eGFR (52 mL/min = stage 3a), not from today's AKI-elevated creatinine. Both N17.9 and N18.31 are coded simultaneously per ICD-10-CM guidelines; AKI and CKD are distinct conditions and neither subsumes the other. Do not upgrade the CKD stage (e.g., to N18.4) based on an acutely elevated creatinine — the CKD stage reflects the chronic baseline.

note: 34-year-old woman presenting with an 8-day history of right-sided facial pressure and pain, nasal congestion, purulent yellow nasal discharge, and low-grade fever to 37.9°C. Symptoms began 3 days after a viral upper respiratory infection. No prior history of sinusitis or sinus surgery. Tenderness to percussion over the right maxillary sinus. CT sinuses: right maxillary sinus opacification with air-fluid level; left maxillary, frontal, and ethmoid sinuses clear. Symptom duration less than 4 weeks. No causative organism identified. Diagnosis: acute maxillary sinusitis. Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875mg BID for 10 days prescribed.
notes: J01.00 = acute maxillary sinusitis, unspecified organism — symptom duration of 8 days (under 4 weeks) and a de novo presentation confirm the acute designation. Do not use J32.0 (chronic maxillary sinusitis), which requires symptoms persisting ≥12 weeks; 8 days does not meet that threshold. J01.01 (recurrent acute maxillary sinusitis) requires documented recurrence, which is absent here. No causative pathogen was confirmed, so a pathogen-specific code is not applicable. Only the right maxillary sinus is affected — do not code other sinuses as they are clear on CT.

note: 45-year-old woman presenting for 5-week follow-up after a nondisplaced fracture of the left lateral malleolus sustained in a fall down two steps. Initial treatment: short leg cast and non-weight-bearing instructions. No surgical intervention. X-ray today: fracture in maintained alignment with visible periosteal callus formation; no secondary displacement. Minimal residual tenderness. Cast removed; transitioning to a removable walking boot for an additional 2 weeks followed by supervised physical therapy for ankle rehabilitation. Subsequent encounter for fracture with routine healing.
notes: S82.606D = nondisplaced fracture of lateral malleolus of left fibula, subsequent encounter for fracture with routine healing. The 7th character encodes the care phase: 'A' = initial encounter (all visits during active treatment, including casting and reduction); 'D' = subsequent encounter once the fracture is in the routine healing phase; 'G' = subsequent with delayed healing; 'K' = subsequent with nonunion; 'P' = subsequent with malunion; 'S' = sequela. Visible callus on X-ray confirms routine healing — use 'D', not 'G'. Do not re-assign 'A' simply because this is the patient's first visit to this provider; the fracture has been in active treatment for 5 weeks, placing it in the subsequent-encounter phase.

note: 33-year-old woman with known mild persistent asthma (baseline SABA use 2–3 times per week) presenting to urgent care with a 2-day acute worsening of wheezing and chest tightness requiring albuterol use every 4 hours without adequate relief. O2 saturation 94% on room air, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min. Diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally. Peak expiratory flow 68% of personal best. Treated with three rounds of albuterol nebulization and a single dose of oral prednisone 40mg. Post-treatment: O2 saturation 97%, peak flow 83% of personal best, wheezing resolved. Discharged with 5-day prednisone taper and short-acting bronchodilator. Diagnosis: mild persistent asthma with acute exacerbation.
notes: J45.31 = mild persistent asthma with acute exacerbation. The provider documents both the underlying severity (mild persistent: SABA use 2–3 days/week at baseline) and an acute exacerbation as the reason for the visit — both elements must appear in the code. Do not use J45.30 (mild persistent, uncomplicated) when an exacerbation is explicitly documented; J45.30 is for stable visits without an acute event. Do not use J45.41 (moderate persistent with exacerbation) — the underlying asthma severity classification is mild persistent regardless of the acute episode's clinical intensity. Compare with ex011 (J45.40, moderate persistent, no exacerbation) and ex021 (J45.30, mild persistent, no exacerbation).
